name: Kuchisake-onna
alias: The Slit-Mouthed Woman · a masked figure who asks if she is pretty, and kills you for either answer
body: First you notice the mask, a surgical white that floats at the edge of a sodium streetlight, and then the voice, soft and almost shy, asking whether you think she is pretty. She is alone on a quiet road at the hour when the last train has gone and the convenience stores have thinned to a single humming sign, the asphalt still damp, the vending machines glowing blue against shuttered shops. She wears a long pale coat, and her eyes above the cloth are dark and patient and entirely steady, fixed on you as though she has all night and you are the only appointment she means to keep. There is nothing visibly wrong with her at all, which is exactly the thing that should make you run; the trap has already closed, quietly, in the politeness of the question itself.

She is a woman wronged, disfigured in life or in a jealous rage, in some tellings cut by the slip of a surgeon's blade, and she returns to put her question to anyone she finds walking alone. If you say she is ugly, she cuts you down where you stand. If you say she is pretty, she draws the mask away to show a mouth slit from ear to ear, a wet red grin held open by old wounds, and she leans close and asks once more: am I pretty now? The honest answer and the flattering answer are both confessions, and she carries scissors, or in some regions a sickle or a long blade, to collect on either. The only seams in her trap are ambiguity and delay. Tell her you are not sure, that she is only average; ask her a question of your own; say you have somewhere to be, which may make her bow and apologise out of the manners she still keeps; throw a handful of bekkou-ame or other hard candy so that she stoops to gather it, a trick borrowed from older fox and ghost lore where greedy spirits are slowed by scattered things; or say the word for hair pomade, repeated three or six times, a charm said to recall the doctor who ruined her and to buy you the second you need to reach the corner.

What lingers is the courtesy of it, the way she waits to be answered rather than simply striking, as though manners were the last human thing she kept. People who claim to have met her remember the question far longer than the mouth. The tale belongs to the kaidan tradition of whispered horror passed mouth to mouth on the dark walk home, and in 1979 it broke loose into a true nationwide panic: beginning near Yaotsu in Gifu, the rumour ran through the new cram schools that gathered children from scattered neighbourhoods, who carried it back along telephone lines until schools arranged group escorts, parents formed patrols, and the police thickened their rounds across whole prefectures. It surfaced again around 2004, and still children walking home in small clusters chant the escape words under their breath, and somewhere a parent has told them that if the woman ever asks, you answer with another question and you do not look back at the scissors at her side.
note: The classic wards are stalling tactics rather than true protection: an ambiguous reply such as "you are average" confuses her long enough to flee, and the word "pomade" (said three or six times in some tellings) is a charm tied to the surgeon of her origin story. Throwing bekkou-ame or other hard candy makes her stop to pick it up, a trick borrowed from older fox and ghost lore where greedy spirits are delayed by scattered objects.

Kuchisake-onna surged into a nationwide panic across Japan in 1979, when rumours spread through schoolchildren that she chased her victims at supernatural speed, and police and teachers fielded real reports. She revived again around 2004 and travels in regional variants: some give her a sickle or a scythe instead of scissors, some place her near schools or tunnels, and Korean retellings echo the same masked question. The tale belongs to the kaidan tradition of urban whispered horror, passed mouth to mouth on the walk home after dark.

recountings.text: The best known account is the 1979 mass panic that began in Yaotsu, Gifu prefecture, where an elderly farm woman with a marked face stood in her garden at night and frightened children mistook her for the slit-mouthed woman. Local then national press amplified the story, and within months children refused to walk alone, schools arranged group escorts home, parents formed patrols, and police increased their rounds across several prefectures. This is widely-circulated lore and reported social history, not a single person's testimony.

Folklore scholar Iikura Yoshiyuki explains how the rumor traveled so fast: newly popular cram schools gathered children from different neighborhoods, who carried the story back to their own schools, where it spread by word of mouth and telephone until magazines picked it up. He argues the slit-mouthed woman counts as the first distinctly Japanese urban legend, predating the term itself in Japan. This is a sourced scholarly analysis rather than a personal sighting.

The standard retelling of the encounter has her approach in a surgical mask and ask 'Watashi, kirei?' (Am I pretty?); if you say no she kills you, and if you say yes she pulls down the mask, reveals a mouth slit ear to ear, and asks again before attacking with scissors. Popular escape tricks include calling her looks average to buy time, claiming you have an appointment so she politely apologizes, throwing hard candy, or repeating the word pomade three times. This is widely-circulated lore with many local variants, not one person's account.

references.title: Japanese Urban Legends from the Slit-Mouthed Woman to Kisaragi Station
Kuchisake-onna: legend and history of the slit-mouthed woman
Kuchisake Onna: The Slit-Mouthed Woman of Modern Japan
